title: Open Files
summary: Lanes of invasion for your rooks

steps.title: Why Rooks Love Open Files
steps.text:
## Open and half-open files — defined

- **Open file** — a file with **no pawns of either colour**. Both rooks have an unobstructed lane.
- **Half-open file** — a file with **no pawn of your colour** but at least one **enemy pawn**. Your rook has a target; the opponent's rook is blocked.
- **Closed file** — a file with **friendly pawns** in the way. Your rook on it is asleep.

### What this position is

The pawns have melted away — only c, f, g, h-pawns remain on each side, with d- and e-files **fully open**. Both kings are still in the centre. Whoever **claims** the open files first will have the freer hand.

### Why files matter so much

A rook is a **slow piece** until you give it an open file. On a closed file it does almost nothing. On an open file it threatens **invasion** — penetration to the **7th rank** is one of the most decisive ideas in chess.

### How to USE open files

1. **Identify the open and half-open files** at every stage.
2. **Bring rooks to those files** as soon as development allows.
3. **Contest** the file — if both rooks land on it, **trade** rooks only when you keep a follow-up rook ready (doubling).
4. **Penetrate** to the 7th rank (or 2nd from your perspective) — the *pigs on the 7th* idea.
5. On a **half-open file**, attack the enemy pawn with rook + queen.

### What signs tell you to apply it

- A pawn break has just **opened a file** — race to claim it.
- The opponent has a **backward pawn on a half-open file** — pile pieces on it.
- The enemy king is **on the same file** as your rook with no pawn cover.

### Common mistakes

- Putting **only one rook** on the open file when the opponent contests it — you lose the trade.
- Leaving the **a- and h-files** unclaimed; rim files matter when they lead to the king.
- **Trading rooks** on the open file when your remaining rook is worse than the enemy's.

> A rook on an open file is a piece. A rook on a closed file is a target.

steps.title: Doubling & The 7th Rank
steps.text:
## Doubling rooks — and the 7th rank

The most powerful claim on an open file is **two rooks stacked** on it. Doubling rooks does three things:

1. **Wins** the file in any rook trade — the second rook recaptures and dominates.
2. **Triples** the threat to invade the 7th rank.
3. **Coordinates** with a queen for a heavy-piece battery (a queen between or in front of the rooks creates *Alekhine's gun*).

### Pigs on the 7th

A rook on the **7th rank** (or 2nd from the defender's perspective) is so strong it earned its own nickname — the *pig*. Two rooks doubled on the 7th are *blind pigs* (they trample everything).

Why:

- They attack **pawns on the 7th**, all undefended by other pawns.
- They cut off the **enemy king** to the back rank.
- They threaten **mate** on the 8th rank if the king cannot escape.

### How to USE this in your games

1. **Plan the doubling** — first rook to the file, second rook prepared to follow (often via a rook lift).
2. **Trade off defenders** of the 7th rank before invading.
3. **Co-ordinate with the queen** — the queen + rook on the 7th is a typical mating attack.
4. **Combine with weak pawns** — if all enemy pawns sit on the 7th rank, doubled rooks gobble them.

### Two visual examples in prose

- *Capablanca's classic technique*: rooks on c1/c2 contesting the c-file; opponent disputes; rook trade favours White; second rook follows; **Rc7** then **Rxc7** — the file becomes a winning highway.
- *Alekhine's gun*: queen on c1 in front of both rooks on c2 and c3 (or any vertical stack). The opponent cannot contest the file; one penetration ends the game.

### What signs tell you to apply it

- An open file points **toward the enemy king** or **a weak pawn**.
- The opponent has **only one rook ready** to contest the file — your doubling will win it.
- The 7th rank has **multiple targets**.

### Common mistakes

- **Doubling on the wrong file** — the file with no targets is wasted heavy-piece firepower.
- **Penetrating to the 7th** before the rook is supported — a single rook on the 7th can be trapped.
- **Trading queens** when the queen was the partner — the rooks alone cannot mate without an open king.

> Two rooks doubled on the 7th rank do not win — they finish.
